section.title: Unfaithful Israel Called to Repent
section.sectionRationale: This unit turns from indictment to invitation. Having named Judah as more faithless than her already-judged sister Israel, the LORD Himself calls out, return, and pictures a genuine national confession, before closing with the demand that any return be more than words: an inward circumcision of the heart.
section.thesis: Though Judah has proven even more faithless than exiled Israel, the LORD calls both to return, pictures the honest confession true repentance requires, and insists that only a circumcised heart, not mere ritual or words, will avert the coming wrath.
section.frameNote: Four movements: faithless Israel and treacherous Judah compared; the LORD's own call to return; a voice of confession on the barren heights; and the demand to circumcise the heart before disaster falls.

units.label: Faithless Israel, treacherous Judah
units.note: the sister who watched and did not learn

verses.text: Now in the days of King Josiah, the LORD said to me, “Have you seen what faithless Israel has done? She has gone up on every high hill and under every green tree to prostitute herself there.
I thought that after she had done all these things, she would return to Me. But she did not return, and her unfaithful sister Judah saw it.
She saw that because faithless Israel had committed adultery, I gave her a certificate of divorce and sent her away. Yet that unfaithful sister Judah had no fear and prostituted herself as well.
Indifferent to her own infidelity, Israel had defiled the land and committed adultery with stones and trees.
Yet in spite of all this, her unfaithful sister Judah did not return to Me with all her heart, but only in pretense,” declares the LORD.
And the LORD said to me, “Faithless Israel has shown herself more righteous than unfaithful Judah.

units.label: The LORD's own call to return
units.note: shepherds after His heart, if only they will come

verses.text:
Go, proclaim this message toward the north: 
‘{{shuv|Return}}, O faithless Israel,’ declares the LORD. ‘I will no longer look on you with anger, for I am merciful,’ declares the LORD. ‘I will not be angry forever.

Only acknowledge your guilt, that you have rebelled against the LORD your God. You have scattered your favors to foreign gods under every green tree 
and have not obeyed My voice,’” declares the LORD.
“Return, O faithless children,” declares the LORD, “for I am your master, and I will take you, one from a city and two from a family, and bring you to Zion.
Then I will give you shepherds after My own heart, who will feed you with knowledge and understanding.”

“In those days, when you multiply and increase in the land,” declares the LORD, “they will no longer discuss the ark of the covenant of the LORD. It will never come to mind, and no one will remember it or miss it, nor will another one be made.

At that time Jerusalem will be called The Throne of the LORD, and all the nations will be gathered in Jerusalem to honor the name of the LORD. They will no longer follow the stubbornness of their evil hearts.
In those days the house of Judah will walk with the house of Israel, and they will come together from the land of the north to the land that I gave to your fathers as an inheritance.

units.label: A voice on the barren heights

verses.text: Then I said, ‘How I long to make you My sons and give you a desirable land, the most beautiful inheritance of all the nations!’ I thought you would call Me ‘Father’ and never turn away from following Me.
But as a woman may betray her husband, so you have betrayed Me, O house of Israel,” declares the LORD.
A voice is heard on the barren heights, the children of Israel weeping and begging for mercy, because they have perverted their ways and forgotten the LORD their God.

“Return, O faithless children, and I will heal your faithlessness.” 
“Here we are. We come to You, for You are the LORD our God.
Surely deception comes from the hills, and commotion from the mountains. Surely the salvation of Israel is in the LORD our God.
From our youth, that shameful god has consumed what our fathers have worked for, their flocks and herds, their sons and daughters.

Let us lie down in our shame; let our disgrace cover us. We have sinned against the LORD our God, both we and our fathers; from our youth even to this day we have not obeyed the voice of the LORD our God.”

units.label: Circumcise your hearts
units.note: true repentance is inward before it is outward

verses.text: “If you will return, O Israel, return to Me,” declares the LORD. “If you will remove your detestable idols from My sight and no longer waver,
and if you can swear, ‘As surely as the LORD lives,’ in truth, in justice, and in righteousness, then the nations will be blessed by Him, and in Him they will glory.”

For this is what the LORD says to the men of Judah and Jerusalem: 
“Break up your unplowed ground, and do not sow among the thorns.

{{mul_lev|Circumcise yourselves}} to the LORD, and remove the foreskins of your hearts, 
O men of Judah and people of Jerusalem. Otherwise, My wrath will break out like fire and burn with no one to extinguish it, 
because of your evil deeds.”

shuv.gloss: To turn, return, repent. Repeated insistently across this passage, it is Jeremiah's central word for the change God actually requires: not a ritual gesture but a full turning back of life, worship, and loyalty toward the LORD.

mul_lev.gloss: To circumcise the heart. Physical circumcision marked outward covenant membership; here God demands the inward reality it was always meant to signify, a heart cut free from stubborn self-rule, language later picked up by Moses, Paul, and the promise of a new covenant.

basic.context: God compares Judah unfavorably to her already-exiled sister, the northern kingdom Israel: Israel sinned and was sent away, yet Judah watched that judgment and sinned even more brazenly, offering only a pretend repentance. And yet, astonishingly, the LORD Himself keeps calling, return, promising mercy and even picturing a scene where the people finally confess honestly, before insisting that real change must be internal.
basic.hermeneutics: This is prophetic covenant language using a real historical comparison, the earlier fall of the northern kingdom, to make a moral point about Judah's greater guilt. The honest hard question is why God pictures a confession in 3:21-25 that the rest of the book shows Judah never actually makes; the answer is that this is an invitation and a template for genuine repentance, not a report that it happened, and Judah's continuing unfaithfulness afterward proves the invitation was real but refused.
basic.originalLanguages: Shuv, return, tolls through this passage like a summons, God's own repeated plea rather than a demand issued once and withdrawn. The passage closes with the demand to circumcise, mul, the heart, taking the covenant sign that marked Israel outwardly and insisting it must cut something inward, stubborn self-rule, or it means nothing.
basic.typology: The call to circumcise the heart anticipates Moses' own promise in Deuteronomy 30:6 that God Himself will one day circumcise His people's hearts, a promise fulfilled in the new covenant and the gift of the Spirit, Romans 2:29. The gathering of scattered Israel and Judah under shepherds after God's own heart anticipates the one Shepherd, Christ, who unites His scattered flock.
basic.christ: Paul explicitly redefines true circumcision as a matter of the heart, by the Spirit, not the letter, Romans 2:29, fulfilling exactly what Jeremiah 4:4 demands but the Old Covenant could not produce apart from the Spirit poured out through Christ. Jesus is the shepherd after God's own heart promised in 3:15, gathering one flock from Israel and the nations, John 10:16.
basic.god: God is patient beyond what Judah's repeated failures deserve, calling return again and again even after naming her guilt plainly and comparing her unfavorably to a sister already judged. Yet His patience is not indifference: the passage ends with a real warning that unextinguished wrath will fall if the heart itself is not changed.
basic.crossReferences: Deuteronomy 30:6 promises that the LORD Himself will circumcise Israel's heart to love Him fully. Romans 2:28-29 defines true circumcision as inward, of the heart, by the Spirit. Ezekiel 36:26-27 promises a new heart and God's own Spirit within His people. John 10:16 has Jesus gather one flock under one shepherd, echoing 3:15 and 3:18.
basic.oneStory: This is God's patient, repeated call to a people who keep answering with pretense rather than real change, insisting that true repentance must reach the heart itself. That demand, met only partly under the old covenant, is finally fulfilled when God gives His people new hearts and His own Spirit through Christ, the good shepherd who gathers His scattered flock.

context.literary: The passage moves from a historical comparison (3:6-11) into direct divine speech calling for return (3:12-18), then a modeled confession voiced for the people (3:19-25), and closes with a conditional call to genuine repentance (4:1-4), forming a rhetorical arc from indictment to invitation to demand.
context.historical: Set explicitly in the days of Josiah (3:6), this oracle looks back on the northern kingdom's fall to Assyria roughly a century earlier as a cautionary example Judah failed to heed, even while Josiah's own reforms were underway, showing that outward reform without inward change was already a live danger in Jeremiah's day.
context.canonical: The demand to circumcise the heart draws directly on Deuteronomy 10:16 and 30:6, connecting Jeremiah's call to the Torah's own vision of true covenant faithfulness, and anticipates the fuller promises of inward renewal in Ezekiel and the New Testament.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To show that Judah's sin is worse than the sin that already brought judgment on her sister Israel, while still extending, in God's own voice, a genuine and repeated invitation to return, provided that return reaches the heart and not merely outward behavior.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The comparison between Israel and Judah, and the specific historical circumstances of divided kingdoms, is descriptive of a particular moment; it does not prescribe ongoing judgments between nations. The prescriptive, enduring truth is that repentance God accepts must be inward, a circumcised heart, not merely outward ritual or words.

debates.question: Is the confession in 3:21-25 a report of Israel's actual repentance, or an invitation modeling what true repentance should look like?

views.label: A rhetorical model, not an accomplished fact
views.case: The rest of Jeremiah shows Judah continuing in unfaithfulness, so this confession functions as a template God puts in the people's mouths, showing them what honest repentance would sound like, rather than reporting that it actually occurred.

views.label: A genuine but partial or temporary repentance under Josiah
views.case: Some link this passage to real reforming movements under Josiah's reign, seeing at least a partial, historically located response of humility, even if it did not last or reach every heart.

debates.bearing: Either way, the passage teaches that God delights to hear this kind of honest confession and stands ready to heal it, while the continuing narrative of Jeremiah shows how rarely such words were matched by lasting change.

study.originalLanguages: God's summons rests on shuv, return, repeated as both invitation and command, insisting on an actual turning of life rather than a change of vocabulary. The unit ends demanding that the covenant sign of circumcision, mul, be applied to the heart itself, stripping away stubborn self-rule that no outward ritual could touch on its own.

typology.type: The demand to circumcise the heart, Jeremiah 4:4
typology.fulfillment: Fulfilled in the new covenant gift of a circumcised heart by the Spirit, promised in Deuteronomy 30:6 and Ezekiel 36:26-27, and realized through Christ, Romans 2:29.

typology.type: Shepherds after God's own heart gathering scattered Israel and Judah, Jeremiah 3:15-18
typology.fulfillment: Christ, the good shepherd who lays down His life for the sheep and gathers one flock from Israel and the nations, John 10:11-16.

study.christ: Paul takes up this passage's demand almost verbatim, insisting that real circumcision is a matter of the heart, by the Spirit, not the letter, Romans 2:29, showing that what Jeremiah 4:4 required but the old covenant could not fully produce is accomplished only through the Spirit given in Christ. Jesus is Himself the shepherd after God's own heart promised in 3:15, the one shepherd who at last gathers the scattered flock of Israel and the nations into one, John 10:16.
study.god: God's patience runs far beyond what Judah's repeated, brazen unfaithfulness deserves; He calls return again even after naming her sin more severe than her already-judged sister's. Yet He is not a God who accepts empty gestures: His mercy is real, but it is aimed at a genuinely changed heart, and unextinguished wrath still waits if that change never comes.

crossReferences.note: Promises that the LORD Himself will one day circumcise Israel's heart so they can love Him fully, anticipating what Jeremiah 4:4 demands.
Paul defines true circumcision as inward, of the heart, by the Spirit, fulfilling Jeremiah's demand.
Promises a new heart and God's own Spirit placed within His people, the mechanism by which the circumcised heart becomes possible.
Jesus gathers one flock under one shepherd, echoing the promise of shepherds after God's own heart in Jeremiah 3:15-18.

study.oneStory: This is God's patient, repeated call to a people who answer with pretense more often than genuine change, insisting from the start that real repentance must cut to the heart. That demand finds its full answer only when God gives His people new hearts through the Spirit poured out in Christ, the good shepherd who gathers His scattered flock at last.

quiz.q: How does God compare Judah to her sister Israel in this passage?

options.text: Judah has proven even more faithless, sinning further despite watching Israel's judgment.
options.note: Verse 11 explicitly states that faithless Israel has shown herself more righteous than treacherous Judah.

options.text: Judah was innocent while Israel alone was guilty.
options.note: The passage explicitly indicts Judah as worse, not innocent.

options.text: Both were equally faithful to the covenant.
options.note: Neither kingdom is described as faithful; Judah is described as the more unfaithful of the two.

options.text: Judah repented while Israel refused to.
options.note: The passage says the opposite: Judah only pretended to return, verse 10, while Israel's judgment had already fallen.

quiz.q: What does God repeatedly call His people to do in Jeremiah 3:12-22?

options.text: Return, promising mercy and healing rather than endless anger.
options.note: The Hebrew shuv, return, is repeated as the LORD's own invitation, paired with the promise, I will heal your faithlessness.

options.text: Rebuild the temple immediately.
options.note: The call is to return in heart and loyalty, not a building project.

options.text: Seek alliances with Egypt and Assyria.
options.note: Such alliances are condemned elsewhere in Jeremiah, not commanded here.

options.text: Wait silently for judgment without any response.
options.note: The passage actively calls for a response, return, not passive silence.

quiz.q: What does the confession in Jeremiah 3:21-25 model?

options.text: Honest acknowledgment of guilt and shame rather than excuses.
options.note: The voice says, let us lie down in our shame, we have sinned, a template of unflinching honesty before God.

options.text: A demand that God explain Himself before they will repent.
options.note: The confession contains no demand of God; it is pure acknowledgment of guilt.

options.text: Blaming their ancestors entirely and excusing themselves.
options.note: The confession includes both we and our fathers, not shifting blame away from the speakers themselves.

options.text: A promise to build more altars to foreign gods.
options.note: This is the opposite of the passage's point; the confession renounces, not embraces, idolatry.

quiz.q: What does God demand in Jeremiah 4:4, and why does it matter?

options.text: Circumcising the heart, showing that real repentance must be inward, not merely outward.
options.note: This demand insists true change must reach beneath ritual and words to the heart itself.

options.text: Literal circumcision performed publicly as a display.
options.note: Circumcision was already an established covenant sign; the demand here is explicitly that it be applied to the heart, not merely the body.

options.text: Building new altars in every town.
options.note: The passage calls for removing idols, not building new worship sites.

options.text: Paying a temple tax to avert judgment.
options.note: The passage is about inward repentance, not a financial transaction.

quiz.q: How does the New Testament fulfill the demand to circumcise the heart?

options.text: Paul teaches that true circumcision is of the heart, by the Spirit, fulfilled through Christ.
options.note: Romans 2:28-29 directly answers Jeremiah's demand, showing it fulfilled through the Spirit given in Christ.

options.text: It has no connection to anything in the New Testament.
options.note: Paul explicitly develops this very theme in Romans 2.

options.text: It refers only to circumcision requirements for gentile converts.
options.note: Paul's point is broader: true circumcision is inward and spiritual for all believers, not merely a debate over gentile practice.

options.text: It is fulfilled only in a future, still-unrealized age.
options.note: Paul presents the circumcised heart as a present reality through the Spirit, not merely a future hope.

questions: God says Judah watched Israel's judgment and sinned anyway, even more brazenly. What warnings from others' consequences have you seen and ignored in your own life?
God calls return again and again, promising mercy rather than endless anger. Where do you assume God's patience with you has run out, when His own words here say otherwise?
The pictured confession is honest: we have sinned, both we and our fathers. Where are you tempted to explain away sin rather than simply naming it?
God demands a circumcised heart, not merely outward compliance. Where in your life have you settled for looking repentant rather than actually being changed?

This passage insists that real repentance reaches the heart before it reaches behavior. What would it look like this week to let God change your heart first, rather than trying to fix your behavior on your own?

translationNotes.note: The certificate of divorce alludes to Deuteronomy 24:1-3, formal legal language for ending a marriage, applied metaphorically to the LORD's judgment on the northern kingdom's exile.

The statement that the ark of the covenant will no longer be missed or remade points to a future so transformed by God's presence that the old symbol of that presence becomes unnecessary, not a devaluing of the ark in its own time.

Circumcise yourselves to the LORD uses the covenant sign of Genesis 17 metaphorically; the demand is for the heart itself to be cut free from stubbornness, language Moses had already used in Deuteronomy 10:16.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched via the Makor scripture toolkit this session.
